step1.title: Hi! Meet your AI team
step1.body: Seven roles — team-lead, backend, frontend, QA, architect, DevOps, tech-writer — collaborate on tasks as a single team. A quick minute to show you how it works.

step2.title: Tasks flow through columns
step2.body: Tasks move from «To do» → «In progress» → «Review» → «Done». Each column reflects the current state of work.

step3.title: Click a card for details
step3.body: Clicking a task card opens its details. Coloured chips show the priority, assigned role, and the LLM model being used.

step4.title: Create tasks yourself
step4.body: Click «+ New task», describe what needs doing. The team-lead will break it down and delegate.

step5.title: Inbox — your action queue
step5.body: Everything that needs your attention lands here: approvals, team questions, and work ready to be signed off.

step6.title: Statistics — team metrics
step6.body: Token usage, model costs, activity heatmap — everything about how the team is spending its time and budget.

step7.title: Roles — your team roster
step7.body: See who is on your team. You can add custom roles or edit existing ones — each role is a markdown system prompt.

step8.title: Archive — completed work
step8.body: Done tasks older than 7 days live here. Nothing is deleted — the history is always accessible.

step9.title: Settings — language, theme, team
step9.body: Change the interface language, colour theme, auto-mode limits, and team-lead expertise level.

step10.title: Launch the team-lead
step10.body: ▶ Start team — the team-lead reads the board, picks the next task and starts delegating. «⏹ Stop» ends the session.

step11.title: Auto mode
step11.body: 🤖 Auto — the team-lead starts the next session on their own whenever there are tasks in the queue. No need to click «Start» every time.

step12.title: Chat with the team-lead
step12.body: Leave messages here — the team-lead reads them at the start of each session and reports back. This is your async communication channel.

intro.title: Introduction
intro.body:
What is Devboard

Devboard is your personal AI development team running on your own computer. You are the owner. You have seven virtual team members — team-lead, backend, QA, architect, frontend, DevOps, and tech-writer. You write tasks on the board; the team-lead routes them to the right role.

In v2.0 you can create new departments — marketing, design, or anything else — using the HR role.

How it works

  1. Create a task on the board.
  2. Click ▶ Start team.
  3. The team-lead reads the board, breaks down the task, and delegates to the right role.
  4. When work is done, the task appears in your Inbox for sign-off.
  5. Click ✓ Accept — task moves to the archive.

What Devboard does not do

  • Does not run code in production on its own — it only writes code.
  • Does not deploy without your explicit approval.
  • Does not make phone calls.

tasks.title: How to write tasks
tasks.body:
Five rules for a good task

  1. Title: verb + object. Write "Add X", "Fix Y", "Remove Z" — not "task about X".
  2. Description: TL;DR on line one, details below.
  3. Acceptance: a measurable checklist. List exactly what must work when the task is done.
  4. Keep tasks small. More than 5 acceptance criteria? Split into multiple tasks.
  5. Set a priority: P1 = urgent, P2 = important, P3 = whenever.

Good example

✅ Title: Add About page with team profiles

Description:
The site needs an /about page. Show the team — 4 people: Ivan (CEO), Anna (CTO), Oleg (Backend), Maria (Design). Match the style of the existing /pricing page.

Acceptance:

  • /about loads without errors
  • 4 profile cards are visible with names and roles
  • Page is responsive on mobile

Why this works: the title is specific, description explains what and where, acceptance criteria are measurable.

Bad examples

❌ Title: Make a website

Why this fails: no clarity on which site, for what purpose, or what done looks like. The team-lead has nowhere to start.

❌ Title: fix bug
Description: not working

Why this fails: which bug, where, under what conditions? The team-lead has to guess.

departments.title: How to create departments
departments.body:
When do you need a new department?

When your work goes beyond software development.

  • Marketing — blog posts, social content, audience research.
  • Design — banners, illustrations, landing pages.
  • Support — answering user questions and handling tickets.

When you do NOT need a new department

  • If the task is development work — use the existing dev department.
  • If it is a one-off task — create it inside dev, not a separate department.

How to create a department

  1. Click + Department in the sidebar.
  2. A modal opens: enter a name, description, and optionally pick a template.
  3. HR proposes a team plan. Edit it, then activate.
  4. Once activated, the department appears in the sidebar.

Available templates

Five ready-to-use templates: Marketing, Design, Sales, Support, Operations. Start from a template and tell HR "remove X, add Y" to customise.

hr.title: Working with HR role
hr.body:
Why a good brief matters

HR is smart, but not a mind-reader. The more clearly you describe the department, the better the team plan you get.

Good brief

Department name: Marketing
Description: Content team for a developer-focused startup. Audience: Russian-speaking developers, 25–35. Tone: friendly, technical. Output: blog posts (1500–2500 words), Twitter/LinkedIn posts, SEO research. Out of scope: video production, graphic design.

Why this works: clear audience, tone, what we produce, and what we explicitly exclude.

Bad brief

Name: Marketing
Description: marketing

HR will generate a generic template-based team — not optimised for your actual use case.

How the edit loop works

HR proposes a plan → you respond with edits → HR revises → you activate. Common edit requests:

  • "Add a Brand Strategist"
  • "Remove Social Media — we are not on social"
  • "Rename Marketing Lead to Head of Growth"

shortcuts.title: Keyboard shortcuts
shortcuts.body:
Keyboard shortcuts available in the dashboard.

KeyAction
EscClose modal
Ctrl / Cmd + EnterSave task in editor
Ctrl / Cmd + KSearch coming soon

step2.title: Step 2: chat with HR
step2.answer_placeholder: Tell HR what to change or confirm the plan…

field.name: Name
field.description_placeholder: What the department does: goals, audience, format…

errors.answer_failed: Failed to send message to HR.
errors.approve_failed: Activation failed. You can edit the plan and try again.
errors.session_lost: HR session lost — try creating the department again.
errors.aborted: HR session aborted. Close the window and try again.

note.line1: Session — one team-lead run. Turn — a prompt→reply pair (subscription limits count these). Input includes cache reads.

mode.human_title: Human-readable only: what the team-lead thinks and does

roles.title: Roles

field.name: Name (slug)
field.nameHint: Lowercase a–z, 0–9, hyphens only. Must start with a letter.
field.nameTooltip: Lowercase a–z, 0–9, hyphens only. Must start with a letter.

step2.title: Tell us about yourself
step2.tech_hint: Technical details, full logs, tool names

step4.body: Everything is configured. Start the onboarding tour or dive right in.
